steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from verb (he scattered) to noun (one who scatters) by adding alif
Added ات to make it refer to multiple feminine things that scatter
Added ال to point to specific scatterers

steps.m: and the ones who scatter (feminine plural)
steps.n: Added و at beginning to mean 'and', and kasra at end to connect with following word

steps.m: to scatter or spread things in the wind

steps.n: Added vowel marks to form the basic verbal noun showing the action itself
Adding tanween alif turns this into an action word showing how something is done - in this case, how the winds scatter things
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Adding alif after first root letter and kasra on middle letter makes it describe who does the action
Adding ات makes it feminine plural, used here for the winds that carry rain clouds (mentioned in وِقْرًا - burdens)
Adding ال makes it specific/definite
Adding فَ connects to previous context. The kasra at the end shows this is part of an oath. The alif is written as a long ā.
Changed the action of being heavy into a thing that is heavy by adding specific vowel marks

steps.m: a heavy load (object being carried by الحاملات)
steps.n: Added ًا to show this is the thing being carried. This word is what's being carried by الحاملات (the carriers) in this verse.
Changed from basic root letters to past tense verb by adding vowel marks (fat-ha) on each letter
Added alif after first letter and kasra after second to show ongoing action
Added ات to make it feminine plural
Added ال to make it definite

steps.m: and by the ones (feminine) that flow smoothly
steps.n: Added فَ ('and by') at the start to make it an oath (like saying 'I swear by'), and changed the ending to match other oath words
Changed the verb into a noun by adding damma (ُ) on first letter and sukun (ْ) on middle letter. This makes it describe the state of being easy or smooth.
Added tanwin alif (ًا) at the end to show this word describes how something is done. In this ayah, it describes how الْجٰرِيٰتِ (the ones that flow) are moving - they flow smoothly/with ease.
Doubling the middle letter shows this happens repeatedly or to many things
Changes the verb 'divided' into 'one who divides' by adding مُ at the start

steps.m: those (feminine) who distribute/divide extensively (doers of أَمْرًا)
steps.n: Adding ات makes it feminine plural. This refers to angels who distribute matters/commands (أَمْرًا).
Adding ال (the) at the start makes it mean 'the specific ones' instead of just 'ones', and adding ِ (kasra) at the end shows it's being used as an object

steps.m: and the female ones who divide
steps.n: Adding conjunction prefix to connect with previous statement
Added sukun on middle letter to make it a noun meaning the thing itself

steps.m: a command/matter/affair (object of فَٱلْمُقَسِّمٰتِ - those who distribute)
steps.n: The ًا ending shows this word is the object being distributed and is indefinite (not specific)
This is a special word used at the start of sentences to make what follows more emphatic or certain. Here it emphasizes that what you are promised is true.
Adding ما changes the meaning from simple emphasis to restriction, showing that what you are promised is the only truth, nothing else.
This root is about making a commitment about something in the future, either good or bad
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he promised' to 'is promised' by adding يُ at start and changing middle and last vowels - makes it passive/receiving the action
Changed from 'he' to 'you' by replacing يُ with تُ at the start

steps.m: you (all) are promised/warned (what is mentioned in this surah)
steps.n: Added ونَ to show this applies to multiple people being addressed
This root is about truth and honesty
This pattern shows someone who does the action regularly - a truth-teller

steps.m: indeed/truly one who tells the truth (predicate of إِنَّ)
steps.n: The laam prefix adds emphasis/intensification to the meaning, reinforcing the truthfulness being described. This is called laam al-tawkeed (emphasis)

steps.m: indeed truthful (predicate of إِنَّ describing what you are promised)
steps.n: The tanween (ٌ) shows this is indefinite. As the predicate of إِنَّ, it describes what you are promised (ما توعدون)
This is an emphasis particle that makes what follows more certain. In this ayah, it emphasizes that the judgment/recompense (ٱلدِّينَ) will definitely happen.

steps.m: and indeed (emphasizes that ٱلدِّينَ is true)
steps.n: The و at the beginning connects this statement with what came before it, showing this is an additional truth being emphasized

steps.m: to judge or repay what is due
steps.n: The meaning evolved from 'debt/obligation' to 'judgment/religion'
Root letters were joined with kasra to make a noun meaning 'way of life' or 'system of beliefs'
Added ال to point to a specific religion/system, and doubled the د letter because it comes after ال
Added fatha at the end because this word is the object of إِنَّ (indeed) in the phrase وَإِنَّ ٱلدِّينَ (and indeed, the religion)
Basic past tense form showing something happened
Changed from basic verb root to active participle form (like doer/one who does) to show it is definitely happening. Related to الدِّينَ (judgment) to show it is occurring for sure.
Added لَ at the beginning to emphasize that it will definitely happen
The ٌ (tanween) shows this is indefinite. The alif changed to a long alif (ٰ) due to writing style.
Adds اء to make it a noun referring to something high/elevated
Added ال with shadda on س to make it specific
Added وَ at the beginning to connect it to what came before

steps.m: and by the sky/heaven (which is described by ذَاتِ ٱلْحُبُكِ - 'having pathways')
steps.n: Added kasra at the end because it's being described by ذَاتِ (having/possessing) which comes right after it. The alif is written with a special mark (آ) in this style.
This is a special word used to show possession or description. It's feminine because it's describing السَّمَاء (the sky) which is feminine.

steps.m: possessor of (describing السَّمَاء, owned by الْحُبُكِ)
steps.n: The kasra at the end shows this word is connected to الْحُبُكِ (the paths/orbits) that comes after it, making it mean 'sky possessing paths'
Added damma marks to create a plural noun meaning pathways or orbits, referring to the beautiful patterns in the sky
Added ال to specify these particular pathways in the sky
Added kasra to show this describes what the sky possesses, connecting it to ذَاتِ (meaning 'having/possessing')
This is a special word that adds emphasis and certainty to what comes after it, like adding 'definitely' or 'certainly' in English
Added كُمْ to show this applies to multiple people being addressed. This word connects to لَفِى (are surely in) which comes next in the ayah.
Basic preposition showing location or state. Here it connects to قَوْلٍ (statement/saying) to show being 'in' different statements.
The لَ adds emphasis because this comes after إِنَّكُمْ (indeed you all), making the statement stronger. Together they mean 'Indeed you are truly in...'
Root letters that convey the basic meaning
Changed from basic root meaning 'say' to the noun 'speech' by adding the vowels 'a' and silent 'w'
Added tanween kasra (ٍ) because this word comes after the preposition في. The tanween also shows this is any speech, not a specific one.

steps.m: to differ or vary from something
steps.n: Root letters that convey the basic meaning
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Added مُ at start and i-vowel in middle to make it mean 'someone who does the action'
Added ت after first letter to show things differing from each other (like colors or opinions)
Added shadda on م to emphasize the meaning
Added tanwin kasra to show this is describing the indefinite noun قَوْلٍ (statement)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changed from past to present tense and made it passive by adding يُ

steps.m: away from (preposition used with يُؤْفَكُ - is turned away)
steps.n: This is a preposition that shows something moving or turning away. In this ayah, it's used with the verb يُؤْفَكُ (is turned away) to show who is being turned away from the truth.

steps.m: away from him (object of يُؤْفَكُ - is turned away)
steps.n: The هُ at the end means 'him'. This shows who is being turned away from. The word connects to يُؤْفَكُ (is turned away) to tell us 'turned away from him'.
This is a special word used to refer to people. In this ayah, it refers to those who are turned away from the truth. It connects to يُؤْفَكُ (is turned away) and أُفِكَ (was deluded).
Connected the letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb

steps.m: he was turned away (subject of يُؤْفَكُ)
steps.n: Changed the vowels to show this happened to someone rather than someone doing it. This word is related to يُؤْفَكُ in the ayah, showing who was turned away.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb

steps.m: he was killed (subject of this statement is ٱلْخَرَّاصُونَ - the liars)
steps.n: Changed the vowels to show this happened to someone rather than someone doing it. This word is about what happened to ٱلْخَرَّاصُونَ (the liars).
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb

steps.m: one who constantly makes baseless guesses
steps.n: The doubled ر and added ا show this person does this action as a habit or characteristic

steps.m: those who constantly make baseless guesses
steps.n: Adding ون makes this a masculine plural for people

steps.m: the ones who constantly make baseless guesses (subject of قُتِلَ - 'cursed be')
steps.n: Adding ال makes it definite (like 'the' in English). This word is the subject of قُتِلَ which means 'cursed be' in this context
Added ن to make it refer to multiple people or things
Added ٱلّ at the beginning to point to specific people. This word connects to سَاهُونَ (heedless) in the ayah, showing who is being described as heedless.
This is a personal pronoun used to refer to a group of male people. In this ayah, it's the subject that سَاهُونَ (heedless) describes.
This is the basic preposition that shows location or position
The ى is added to complete the word's spelling. This preposition connects to the word غَمْرَةٍ (overwhelming confusion) to show they are 'in' this state.
The root gives the basic idea of something being completely covered or overwhelmed
Changed from basic root letters to a noun meaning 'the act of overwhelming'

steps.m: a single instance of being overwhelmed
steps.n: Adding ة at the end makes it refer to one specific instance or state of being overwhelmed

steps.m: in a state of being overwhelmed (describes حال of الَّذِينَ)
steps.n: The tanwin kasra (ةٍ) shows this is describing the state of those mentioned (الَّذِينَ). It's connected to فِى before it to mean 'in a state of'.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Adds alif after س to make it describe someone who does the action
Added ون to show it's talking about multiple people. This word describes 'those' (الَّذِينَ) in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Adding يَ at start and changing vowels shows this is happening now. The sukun (ْ) on س makes it easier to pronounce.

steps.m: they ask (about أَيَّانَ يَوْمُ ٱلدِّينِ - when is the Day of Judgment)
steps.n: Adding ونَ shows multiple people are doing the asking. In this ayah, they are asking specifically about when the Day of Judgment will occur.
This is the basic question word that asks about choices or options
Adding shadda makes the word stronger

Adding ان at the end changes this from asking 'which' to asking specifically about time. The fathas are added to help pronunciation. This word is used in the Quran specifically when asking about important future events like the Day of Judgment (يَوْمُ ٱلدِّينِ)
Connected the letters and added fatha to create the basic noun form
Added damma to show this is the subject of the sentence. This word is connected to الدِّينِ (the Judgment) to form the phrase 'Day of Judgment'
Root letters combine with 'i' sound to make a noun meaning a complete system or way of life
Adding ال makes it specific. The د gets a shadda because it comes after ال

steps.m: a day (object of يُفْتَنُونَ - they will be tested)
steps.n: Connected the root letters with vowel marks to make the basic noun. This word shows when the testing happens.

steps.m: on the day when (they will be tested)
steps.n: Adding fatha at the end shows this word tells us when something happens. It connects to the verb يُفْتَنُونَ (they will be tested).
This is a basic pronoun that refers to the disbelievers being tested in the fire. It's the subject of the verb يُفْتَنُونَ (they are being tested/burned).

This is a basic preposition that shows something is happening upon or over something else. In this ayah, it connects the verb يُفْتَنُونَ (they are tried/tested) with ٱلنَّارِ (the Fire), showing they are being tested upon the Fire.
Changes from basic root to noun meaning 'fire'
Added ال to make it specific
Added kasra at the end because it follows the preposition عَلَى (upon)

steps.m: to test or try (especially with hardship)

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed to present tense passive voice by adding يُ at start and changing vowels. This shows the action is happening to someone rather than someone doing it.

steps.m: they are being tested (on the Fire)
steps.n: Added ون to show multiple people are being tested. This word relates to عَلَى ٱلنَّارِ (on the Fire) in the ayah, showing they are being tested with the Fire.

steps.m: to test or try someone's faith through hardship

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make it a past tense verb
Changed from a verb (he tested) to a noun (a test/trial) by changing vowels and adding ة at the end

steps.m: your trial (referring to the punishment mentioned in ذُوقُوا)
steps.n: Added كُمْ to show this trial belongs to those being addressed. This connects to the command 'taste!' (ذُوقُوا) at the start of the verse.
Basic demonstrative element meaning 'here' or 'this'
Added ذا to specify 'this' for masculine singular objects that are close by
Added alif with overbar (ٰ) and fatha marks to show proper pronunciation. In this ayah, it's pointing to 'your trial' (فِتْنَتَكُمْ) mentioned before.

Added damma (ُ) on first letter to start forming the word
Added ت to make it past tense 'was'. The و drops out because it's a weak letter between two vowels

Added kasra to the letter ب to make it readable
Added the pronoun ه (him/it) with a kasra to show it's connected to the word before it

Added vowels to make basic past tense verb
Added است at beginning to show seeking or wanting to do the action

Added ون to show multiple people are doing the action

steps.m: to protect oneself or guard against something bad
steps.n: Basic past tense form with weak letter ي at the end
Added vowels (fatha) to make it mean 'he protected' in the past tense
Added مُ at start to make it 'someone who', then ت merged with ق making a stronger ق sound (shadda), showing someone who protects themselves from bad things
Added ين to make it plural, showing many people who do this
Added ال to specify these particular God-fearing people. This word is the subject of إِنَّ (indeed/verily) in this ayah.
Basic preposition showing location. In this ayah, it shows the righteous people are located in gardens.
Added kasra (ِ) under ف to show how it's pronounced
Changed ي to ى because it's at the end of the word
Adding the basic vowel marks makes it a past tense verb
Added ة to make it a noun meaning 'a protected place', which came to mean 'garden'
Changed to plural form to show multiple gardens
Added two dots below (ٍ) to show it's the object of 'in/في' - this marks where the action happens

Added tanween kasra to show this is indefinite
Added و with fatha to connect this word to 'gardens' (جَنَّاتٍ) mentioned before it
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb

steps.m: those who are taking (subject of كَانُوا)

The alif is written with a special mark (آ) to show it's pronounced longer. This is just a writing style and doesn't change the meaning.

Added هُمْ at the end to show who received the giving. The small alif (ٰ) is added before هُمْ for pronunciation.
The doubled letter shows this is someone's main role or characteristic - they are the one who truly nurtures and cares
هُمْ means 'their' when added to the end. This word is the subject (doer) of the verb ءَاتَىٰ (gave/granted) in this ayah.
This is a basic word used to emphasize what comes after it, like saying 'truly' or 'certainly' in English
Added هُمْ at the end to show who is being talked about - in this case 'they/them'
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak letter in the past tense

steps.m: they were (describing مُحْسِنِينَ - those who do good)
steps.n: Added وا at the end to show multiple people (they) did the action. This word describes how مُحْسِنِينَ (those who do good) were in the past.
Forms a noun meaning 'before' from the root letters
Added fatha at the end to show this word is connected to ذٰلِكَ (that) in the phrase قَبْلَ ذٰلِكَ (before that)
This is the basic demonstrative pronoun used to point to something nearby
The alif is replaced with a small alif above (ٰ) which helps show this points to something farther away
Adding لِ makes it point to something far away instead of nearby

steps.m: that (far) (points to what came before in the ayah)

Added vowels to make it into a past tense verb showing quality
Changes from 'was good' to 'doer of good' by adding مُ at start and changing middle vowels. Like changing 'ran' to 'runner'.

steps.m: those who do good (object of كَانُوا)
steps.n: Changes from singular (one person) to plural (many people) by adding ين at the end. The ين shows it's the object - the ones being talked about in 'they were'
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak letter in the past tense
Added وا to show multiple people (they) did this action in the past. This word tells us about what 'they' were doing before يَهْجَعُونَ (they sleep)
Added vowels to connect the root letters together
Changed the word from an action to a description

steps.m: a little (describing how much they slept)
steps.n: Adding ًا at the end shows this describes how they did the action (sleeping) in the ayah
This is a particle that shows where something comes from or what it's made of. In this ayah, it connects to الَّيْلِ (the night) to show 'from the night'
Added the kasra vowel mark under م to show how it's pronounced
Added shadda to show the م is pronounced strongly
Added fatha at the end to connect smoothly with the next word الَّيْلِ (the night)
Connected the letters and added vowel marks to make it a proper word
Adding ٱلْ with a shadda makes it specific - 'the night' instead of just any night
The ِ ending shows this word follows مِنَ (from) in the ayah. When you see مِنَ before a word, that word gets a ِ at the end.

steps.m: that which (connects to يَهْجَعُونَ - they sleep)
steps.n: This is a particle that connects to the verb after it (يَهْجَعُونَ). In this ayah, it shows how little they slept.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it past tense
Adding يَ at the start changes it from past (he slept) to present/future (he sleeps)

steps.m: they sleep lightly (describing the righteous people mentioned earlier in the ayah)
steps.n: Adding ون makes it plural for 'they'. This describes how the righteous people would sleep only a little during the night.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic noun form
This form makes the word plural to show multiple dawn times
Added conjunction to connect to previous phrase
Added preposition to show location in time
Added the definite article to specify these particular dawn times

steps.m: and in the dawn times (connected to يَسْتَغْفِرُونَ)
steps.n: Added genitive case ending because it follows the preposition بِ

Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb
Added است at beginning to show seeking/requesting the action
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start, ِ on second root letter, and ُ at end
Added ون to show multiple people doing the action
This is a basic preposition showing location or containment. It's used here to show that something exists within their wealth.
Added the kasra vowel mark under the ف to show how it's pronounced
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it
Changed the final ي to ى with an elongation mark (ٓ) which is a writing style variation but doesn't change the meaning. This preposition connects to 'their wealth' (أَمْوٰلِهِمْ) in the next word.
Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to create the basic singular noun
Added hamza at start and و after first root letter to make plural form
Added kasra at the end because this word follows the preposition في (in)

steps.m: their wealth (in the phrase 'in their wealth')
steps.n: Added هِمْ at the end to show possession by them
Root letters connected with fatha and shadda to make a noun meaning 'truth' from the basic meaning 'to be true'
Adding double damma makes the word indefinite (like 'a' in English)
Added ا after first letter to show who does the action, and added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation

steps.m: and the deprived one (connected to لِلسَّآئِلِ - for the beggar)

steps.m: in (preposition showing location in الْأَرْضِ - the earth)

steps.m: in (preposition showing location in الْأَرْضِ - the earth)

Changed to plural form by adding ات and replacing the ة. The alif is written as a long alif (ٰ) in this form.
Added tanween damma to show this is the subject doing something in the sentence

Added ين to make it plural (more than two)

steps.m: for the ones who are certain
steps.n: Added ال to make it definite (specific)
This is a basic preposition that shows location or containment

Added و at the beginning to mean 'and'. This word shows where أَنفُسِكُمْ (yourselves) is located.

steps.m: your selves (referring to those being addressed)
steps.n: Added كُمْ at the end with ِ before it to show this belongs to the people being spoken to

steps.m: to see or understand with insight

Added ون to show multiple people are doing this action
This is a basic preposition that shows location or containment. In this ayah, it connects to the word ٱلسَّمَآءِ to show where something is located.

steps.m: and in (connects to ٱلسَّمَآءِ - the sky/heaven)
steps.n: The و adds the meaning of 'and' to connect this phrase with what came before. Together with the following words, it forms part of the phrase 'and in the sky is your provision'

steps.m: to rise up or be elevated
steps.n: The root relates to height and elevation
Added fathas to connect the letters into a past-tense verb
Added hamza at the end to make it a noun meaning 'sky'
Added ال at the beginning to make it specific and doubled the س with shadda
Added kasra at the end because this word is connected to رِزْقُكُمْ (your provision) in the ayah

steps.m: to provide with what is needed to live
steps.n: The root relates to provision and sustenance
Changed from an action (providing) to a thing (the provision itself)

steps.m: your (all of you) provision (in السَّمَآءِ/the sky)
steps.n: Added كُمْ to show this provision belongs to multiple people. This provision is mentioned as being in السَّمَآءِ (the sky).
This is a relative pronoun that refers to what is promised, as shown by تُوعَدُونَ (you are promised) later in the ayah

steps.m: and what (that is promised to you)
steps.n: Added و to connect this with the previous phrase about 'your provision is in the sky'
The root relates to making promises or pledges
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form basic past tense verb
Changed vowels to show passive voice - receiving the action instead of doing it
Changed from past tense 'was promised' to present tense 'are promised' by adding تُ at start and changing ending
Added ونَ suffix to show plural (more than one person)

steps.m: lord (one who nurtures and cares for all)
steps.n: The doubled ب (shadda) shows this is someone who nurtures and cares completely
Adds 'and' to connect this word with what came before it
Adds kasra at the end to show this is 'of' the lord
Adds 'so/then' at the start to connect to previous statement
Root meaning 'rise up' became 'sky' by adding اء pattern - makes sense since sky is high up!
Added ٱل to make it specific and doubled the س with shadda
Added ِ to show this belongs to رَبِّ (Lord) in the phrase 'Lord of the sky'
Added vowel marks to connect the letters together
Added 'wa' (and) at start and kasra at end to connect it to 'by the Lord of' in the oath 'by the Lord of heaven and earth'
This is a special word that adds emphasis to what comes after it. It's like adding 'definitely' or 'truly' at the start of a sentence.

steps.m: indeed it (referring to what was sworn by in فَوَرَبِّ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ)
steps.n: Added هُۥ at the end to show what we're talking about. This connects to the next word لَحَقٌّ (truth) to say 'indeed it is truth'.
Added tanween damma to show this is the subject and is indefinite (like 'a' in English)
Added لَ to emphasize that this is definitely true

steps.m: to be similar or equal to something

steps.n: Added vowels to make a noun meaning 'similar to' or 'like'
Added fatha at end to show this word is in accusative case
This is a connecting word that links ideas together, like saying 'what' or 'that which' in English. Here it connects to the word تَنطِقُونَ (you speak) to mean 'what/that which you speak'
The alif is written with a special mark (آ) to show it's pronounced longer, but this doesn't change the meaning
The shadda (ّ) on ن shows emphasis, making the meaning stronger
Added كُمْ to show this applies to multiple people being addressed. This word connects to تَنطِقُونَ (you speak) in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Changed يَ to تَ to show 'you' instead of 'he'
Added ون to show multiple people are speaking
This is a question word that turns the sentence into a yes/no question. In this ayah, it's asking 'Has the story of Ibrahim's honored guests reached you?'
Added fatha marks to show past tense and make the word mean 'he came'

steps.m: he came to you (object of هَلْ - 'has')
steps.n: Added كَ at the end to show who is being addressed. This word is the object of هَلْ (has) in the question 'Has there come to you?'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Changes from verb 'he spoke' to noun 'speech' by adding ي in middle
Connected the root letters with fatha and sukun to make the noun form meaning 'guest'
Added kasra at the end to show this guest belongs to Ibrahim (إِبْرٰهِيمَ) in the phrase 'guest of Ibrahim'
This is a proper name that doesn't come from an Arabic root. It's believed to come from ancient Hebrew meaning 'father of many'
Added fatha at the end to show this is the object of the word حَدِيثُ (story/narrative). In English, we would say 'the story of Abraham'
Added the vowels fatHa (َ) and Damma (ُ) to make 'he was noble' in past tense
Changed to passive participle form showing someone receiving honor
Added plural ending to show multiple people being honored
Added 'the' to make it specific
Added ending to show this word is being acted upon in the sentence
This is a particle that introduces a past event. In this ayah, it introduces when the angels entered upon Ibrahim.
Added fatha marks to connect the letters and show this happened in the past. This is the basic past tense form meaning 'he entered'
Added وا with a damma before it to show that a group of people (they) did the action. The small mark (۟) above وا is just for proper recitation.
This is a basic preposition showing something being above or on top of something else
The ى changes to يْ to prepare for adding the pronoun. This is just a spelling change.

steps.m: upon him (who entered in إِذْ دَخَلُوا۟)
steps.n: Added هِ at the end which means 'him'. This refers back to someone who entered, mentioned in the previous words إِذْ دَخَلُوا۟ (when they entered).
The middle letter و changes to ا when we add the past tense pattern. This is a common change with و in the middle of words.
Added وا to show multiple people did the action. This connects to the previous action in the verse (إِذْ دَخَلُوا۟) - the same group who entered are now speaking.
Added فَ to show this happened right after they entered. The فَ connects this action to إِذْ دَخَلُوا۟ (when they entered) in the previous part of the verse.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Changed to noun form meaning 'peace' by adding alif after second root letter. This is a common form for greetings in Arabic.
Changed the alif to an alif khanjariya (dagger alif) - this is just a writing variation with no meaning change
Added tanween alif to show this is being used as a direct greeting in response to someone
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the letters
Changed و to ا because it's a hollow verb (has weak middle letter)
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Changed verb into noun by adding alif, showing the concept or state of peace itself
Added double dammah to make it an indefinite noun meaning 'a peace'
Changed the middle letter alif to make it longer (ا → ٰ), but means the same thing. Both are ways to write 'Peace!' as a greeting

steps.m: to stand upright, to rise up
a group that stands together (subject of مُّنكَرُونَ)
steps.n: Changed from action to people who do the action. This word is the subject being described as 'unknown' (مُّنكَرُونَ) in this ayah.
Added tanween damma to show this is an indefinite noun (like saying 'a group' instead of 'the group')

steps.m: to deny, reject, or not recognize

steps.n: Changed from root to past tense verb meaning 'he denied/did not recognize'
The shadda makes the meaning stronger and more emphatic

steps.m: unknown ones, strangers (describing the angels who visited Ibrahim)

steps.n: Added vowel marks and changed و to ا. This makes it past tense and shows one person did the action.

steps.m: so he turned away secretly (connected to فَجَآءَ - then he brought)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this action leads to the next action (bringing food). This connects to فَجَآءَ (then he brought) in the ayah.
This is a basic preposition that shows movement or direction towards something
Added an alif maksura (ىٰ) at the end with a small alif above it. This is just a spelling variation and doesn't change the meaning.
Added a small vertical line (ٓ) above the alif maksura. This mark helps connect this word smoothly to the next word أَهْلِهِۦ (his family).
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make the basic noun
Added kasra at the end because it follows the preposition إِلَىٰٓ (to)
Added هِۦ at the end to show it belongs to him
Added fatha marks to show it's past tense and means 'he came'

steps.m: so he came (connected to فَرَاغَ - he turned)
steps.n: The فَ shows this action happened right after he turned to his family

steps.m: to move quickly or be hasty

steps.n: Changed the root into a noun for a specific thing - in this case, a young, quick-moving animal (calf)

steps.m: with a calf (object of جَاءَ - he came)
steps.n: Added بِ at the beginning to mean 'with' and ٍ at the end to show it's any calf (not a specific one). This word is connected to جَاءَ (he came) in the ayah - he came with a calf.
Forms an adjective describing the quality of the calf mentioned in the ayah
The ٍ (tanween kasra) shows this is describing 'a' calf, not 'the' calf
This pattern shows something naturally becoming close/near
Doubling the middle letter makes it mean 'cause something to be close' instead of just 'be close'
Added فَ to show this action happened after something else

steps.m: and then he brought it close (to إِلَيْهِمْ - to them)
steps.n: Added هُ to show what was brought close. This connects to the next word إِلَيْهِمْ which tells us it was brought close 'to them'
This is a preposition showing direction or movement towards something. It doesn't come from a root.
The ى changes to ي before adding pronouns. This is just a spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning.

steps.m: to them (referring to the guests in the story)
steps.n: Added هِمْ which means 'them' when referring to a group of males or mixed group. In this ayah, it refers to the guests being served food.
Added fathas to make it past tense. This is the theoretical form before the weak letter change.

steps.m: he said (introducing what he offered to his guests)
steps.n: The و between two fathas changes to ا. This word introduces what Ibrahim said to his guests after bringing them food.
This is the basic negative word that means 'not'

steps.m: will you not? (asking about تَأْكُلُونَ - eating)
steps.n: Adding أَ at the beginning turns this into a question that expects the answer 'yes'. It's like saying 'Won't you?' in English to encourage someone to do something.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changes from past to present tense by adding تَ and changing vowels
Added ونَ to show multiple people are being addressed

steps.m: to sense or perceive something secretly

steps.n: Added vowel marks to connect the root letters and make it a past tense verb
Added أ at beginning to make it Form IV, which makes the verb take a direct object. Here it takes خِيفَةً (fear) as its object.

steps.m: and then he felt (takes خِيفَةً 'fear' as object)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this feeling happened as a result of what came before in the story
This is a preposition showing where something comes from or starts. The ِ and ْ marks help with pronunciation.

steps.m: from them (referring to the guests/angels in the story)
steps.n: Added هُمْ which means 'them' (for a group of males). In this ayah, it refers back to the guests/angels that Ibrahim was feeling afraid of.
Root letters of 'fear' turned into a noun by adding the 'i' sound in the middle
Added ة to show this is one specific instance of fear

steps.m: a fear (object of أَوْجَسَ - he felt)
steps.n: Added tanween to show this is the object being felt in the phrase 'he felt a fear of them'
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the letters
Changed و to ا because it's a hollow verb

steps.m: they said (to him when he was afraid of them)
steps.n: Adding وا shows that a group of people (the angels) are doing the action. This is their response to Ibrahim's fear mentioned in the previous part of the verse.
This is a negative particle used to tell someone not to do something
Added fatha to show this is being used with a command verb تَخَفْ (fear) to tell someone not to do something. Together لَا تَخَفْ means 'do not fear'
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the past tense verb. The و changes to ا which is common for this type of word.
Added تَ at the beginning to change from 'he' to 'you'
Changed to command form by removing the ا and adding sukun at the end. This word appears after لا (meaning 'do not') to make a negative command.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a past tense verb
Shadda doubles middle root letter to intensify meaning
Adds past tense plural masculine subject marker
ه means 'him' - the person receiving the news

steps.m: and they gave him good news
steps.n: و adds the meaning of 'and' to connect with previous statement
Changes from basic root to noun meaning 'young boy'
Makes the word indefinite (like saying 'a boy' instead of 'the boy')
Changed ending from -un to -in to show the boy is receiving the action (like saying 'a boy' vs 'to/of a boy')
Added بِ (bi) at the start to mean 'with' - like saying 'with a boy' instead of just 'a boy'
Added ي between first and second root letters with vowels to create intensive form showing complete knowledge
Added tanween kasra to show this word is describing the boy (غُلٰمٍ) mentioned in the ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Adding أ at start makes the verb mean 'to come forward' instead of just 'approach'
Added ت with sukun to show feminine past tense
Added kasra to show this is connected to another word coming after it

steps.m: then she came forward (subject of صَكَّتْ and قَالَتْ)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this action follows previous events. This word is the subject of the verbs صَكَّتْ (she struck) and قَالَتْ (she said) in this ayah.
Root letters م-ر-أ got short vowels (َ and ْ) to make the basic word for woman/wife
Added 'i' sound at start to make the word complete - like adding a missing piece
Added feminine marker ة to specify singular feminine noun
Added damma to show this is the subject doing the action in فَأَقْبَلَتِ (she came forward)
Added هُۥ to show possession (his wife) and changed initial alif to wasl form
Basic preposition showing location or containment
Added ي at the end to complete the preposition. This word connects to صَرَّةٍ (crying out) to show where the action happened.
The ي is written as ى when it comes at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Added fatha on first letter and doubled the last letter with shadda to show intensity
Added ة to make it a noun referring to one instance of shouting

steps.m: with a loud cry (describing how امْرَأَتُهُ came)
steps.n: Added tanwin kasra to show this describes how his wife came - with a loud cry

steps.m: to slap/strike with palm of hand

steps.n: Doubling the middle letter ك shows the slap was done with force or intensity
Added ت to show a female did the action (in this case, referring to Abraham's wife)

steps.m: then she slapped/struck forcefully (subject of وَقَالَتْ)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this slapping happened right after she came forward (فَأَقْبَلَتِ) and right before she spoke (وَقَالَتْ)
Added vowel marks to make it a noun referring to the actual face
Added fatha at the end to show this is being acted upon by the verb فَصَكَّتْ (she struck)

steps.m: her face (acted upon by فَصَكَّتْ - she struck)
steps.n: Added ها to show this is her face. This word is what gets struck in the phrase 'she struck her face'
Adding vowel marks to make basic past tense verb
The root قول (to say) changes to قال, then ت is added at the end to show a female said it
Adding و connects this to what happened before
The و makes it mean someone who is very elderly, not just old
Added tanween to show this is indefinite (like 'an' in English). This word is the subject who speaks the next word عَقِيمٌ (barren).

steps.m: to be unable to have children

steps.n: Added ي between second and third root letters to show this is a permanent quality of someone
The ٌ shows this is describing any barren person generally. In this ayah, it's describing عجوزٌ (old woman) who is speaking about herself.

steps.m: he said (subject of رَبُّكِ in next ayah)
steps.n: Added fatha vowels to connect the letters. The و changes to ا because it's between two fathas.
Added وا at the end to show multiple people (they) did the action. The small circle (۟) shows this is a group action.
This is the basic demonstrative word meaning 'this'
Adding ل with kasra changes 'this' to 'that', showing something farther away
Adding كَ at the beginning makes it mean 'like that'
Adding كِ at the end shows this is being said to a female (in this case, to Maryam)
Added fatha marks to show it's past tense and the subject is 'he'
Changed و to ا because it's a hollow verb (has weak middle letter)
Added fatha and doubled the middle letter to show this is someone who does a lot of nurturing
Added ُ to show this word is doing something in the sentence - it's the one saying something

steps.m: your Lord (feminine, as the doer of قَالَ - said)
steps.n: Added كِ at the end to show this is your (feminine) Lord. This Lord is the one who said something in the verse.
Adding shadda changes this from 'if' to 'indeed'. This emphasizes what comes after it.

steps.m: indeed he/it (referring to رَبُّكِ - your Lord)
steps.n: Added هُ at the end to show who is being talked about. Here it refers back to 'your Lord' mentioned earlier.

steps.m: indeed he/it (referring to رَبُّكِ - your Lord)
steps.n: The small و (ۥ) is just a pronunciation helper that shows the هُ should be pronounced clearly.

steps.m: He (referring to Allah in this ayah)
steps.n: This is a personal pronoun that refers back to رَبُّكِ (your Lord) mentioned earlier in the ayah. In this context, it's being used to emphasize that Allah is the one who is wise and knowing.

steps.m: to judge wisely, make wise decisions
one who is full of wisdom
steps.n: Added ي between the last two root letters and vowel marks to show this describes someone who has the quality of wisdom
Added ٱلْ at the beginning to make it definite and ُ at the end to show it's describing Allah. When used as a name of Allah, it means His wisdom is perfect and complete.
Adding vowel marks to make it a past tense verb meaning 'to know'
Changed from past tense 'he knew' to a special form meaning 'one who knows intensely/always' by adding ي
Adding ال makes it 'the' All-Knowing, referring specifically to Allah
Adding damma at the end shows this is the subject of the sentence
Added vowel marks to show this is a complete past-tense verb. This word is connected to the question that follows it (فَمَا خَطْبُكُمْ - 'so what is your mission?')
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in Arabic verbs with و in the middle
Added fatha to show this is an interrogative particle asking about something. Here it's asking about their matter/business (خَطْبُكُمْ).

steps.m: so what (asking about خَطْبُكُمْ - your matter)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this question follows from something before. It connects to the previous word قَالَ (he said), showing this question is a response.
Added vowel marks to make the root into a noun meaning the subject or matter being discussed
Added damma to show this is the main subject being asked about
Added كُمْ to show this matter belongs to or concerns the people being addressed
Added fatha on first letter and shadda on second letter to make the basic calling word
Added damma at the end to show this is the main subject being addressed
Added ها to make it a complete vocative particle for addressing someone
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Added مُ at start to show someone receiving the action (like 'sent one' or 'messenger')
Added ون to show multiple people who are sent

steps.m: the messengers (who are being addressed in this ayah)
steps.n: Added 'ال' (the) to make it 'the messengers' and added 'ون' ending to show it's plural and the subject
Added basic past tense pattern with fatha marks
Changed to common past tense form by adding alif
Added وا at the end to show many people did the action
Added pronunciation marks that don't change the meaning. This word is connected to what comes next - they said 'Indeed, we were sent...'
This is a particle that adds emphasis to a statement, like saying 'truly' or 'certainly' in English

steps.m: indeed we (are the ones who were sent)
steps.n: Added آ at the end to show this is about 'we/us'. This connects to the next word أُرْسِلْنَآ (we were sent) to say 'indeed we were sent'
Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic past tense verb
Added أ at the beginning to make it mean 'to send' rather than just the root meaning
Changed vowels to show this happened to someone/something rather than someone doing it
Added نا at the end to show 'we' were the ones who were sent. The middle letter gets a sukun (ْ) because of this addition.
This is a basic preposition that shows direction or destination. In this ayah, it connects 'we were sent' (أُرْسِلْنَآ) with 'a people' (قَوْمٍ) to show who they were sent to.
Added an alif symbol (ىٰ) at the end to help with pronunciation. This doesn't change the meaning.
Added fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to create a noun meaning 'a group that stands together' - which came to mean 'people' or 'tribe'

steps.m: of a people/tribe (possessed by مُّجْرِمِينَ - criminals)
steps.n: Added tanween kasra to show this word is possessed by or belongs to the criminals mentioned after it
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form basic past tense verb
Added مُ at start and changed vowels to show someone who does this action
Added ين to show multiple criminals. This describes the قوم (people) mentioned in the ayah - they were criminal people.
The shadda (double letter mark) adds emphasis to show these were definitely criminals
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Added نُ at start to show 'we' and changed vowels to show present tense

steps.m: so that we may send (stones upon them)
steps.n: Added لِ (li-) at the start to show 'so that/in order to' and changed the ending to show this is the purpose
This is a basic preposition that shows something is on top of or over something else
Changed the ending to prepare for adding the pronoun

steps.m: upon them (connected to لِنُرْسِلَ - to send)
steps.n: Added هِمْ at the end to show this applies to 'them'. This word connects to لِنُرْسِلَ (to send) showing what is being sent upon.
Connected the root letters with fatha to form the basic noun for a single stone
Changed to plural form by adding alif and changing first vowel to kasra
Adds ةً to show this is the object receiving the action of sending (لِنُرْسِلَ)
This is a connecting word (preposition) that shows something comes from somewhere else. Here it shows the stones are made from clay.
The م gets a shadda (double mark) because it comes after the letter ل in لِنُرْسِلَ. When these letters come together, the ل sound blends into the م sound, making it stronger.
Connected the letters and added kasra to show it's a noun
Added tanween kasra to show this word comes after the word 'from' (مِن) in the phrase 'stones of clay'

steps.m: to mark or brand something with a distinguishing sign
something that has been specially marked
steps.n: Added مُ at start and doubled middle letter to show this is something that receives the action of being marked

steps.m: something that has been specially marked (emphasized)
steps.n: Added emphasis by doubling the م at the beginning

steps.m: something that has been specially marked (feminine)
steps.n: Added ة to make it feminine
Added tanween fatha to show this is the object being sent against the wrongdoers, as mentioned in the surrounding verses
Adding the kasra (ِ) changes this from an action into a word showing location or presence
The fatha at the end shows this word connects to the word after it (رَبِّكَ)
Added fatha on first letter and doubled the middle letter to show complete mastery
Adds second person masculine singular possession. The word عِندَ (with/at) requires what comes after it to be in the genitive case.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added مُ at start and changed vowels to make it describe someone who does this action
Added ين to make it plural for male persons

steps.m: for those who go beyond limits
steps.n: Added لِلْ (for/to the) at beginning and َ at end because it follows the preposition
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Adding أَ at the start makes someone cause the action - instead of going out yourself, you make something else go out
The فَ shows this happened as a result of something before it
Adding نَا changes the subject to 'We' (referring to Allah in this context)
This is a special word that refers to people. Here it means 'whoever' and is the subject of كَانَ (was). It's often used to start a description about people.
Added fatha marks to connect the letters and show this happened in the past

The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two fathas. This is a common change in Arabic that makes the word easier to say. This word connects to فِيهَا (in it) to tell us where something was.
Added kasra under ف to show correct pronunciation

steps.m: in it (referring to the city/town mentioned earlier)
steps.n: في (in) + ها (it/her) = فيها (in it). ها refers back to something feminine mentioned earlier, like a city. The ها is attached to the end.
This is a preposition showing where something comes from or starts, like saying 'from' in English
The َ (fatha) at the end connects it to the next word المؤمنين (the believers), showing we're talking about 'from the believers'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense verb
Added مُ prefix and changed middle vowels to make a word meaning 'someone who believes/trusts'
Added ين to make it plural, showing multiple believers
Added ٱلْ to make it definite and َ at the end because it comes after the preposition مِنَ
This is a basic particle that can mean either 'what' (for questions) or 'not' (for negation). In this ayah, it means 'not' because it's being used with وجدنا (we found) to say 'we did not find'

steps.m: then not (connected to وَجَدْنَا - we found)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from something before. It connects this statement to what happened earlier, like saying 'and so' or 'then'
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the past tense verb
Added sukun to prepare for the suffix
Added نَا at the end to show 'we' did the action. This word is the main verb in this part of the ayah.
Basic preposition meaning 'in'
Added هَا with fatha to show it refers to something feminine mentioned earlier in the context (the city/town). This combines the preposition في (in) with the pronoun ها (it) to show location.

steps.m: to change, to be different from

steps.n: Added fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to create a noun meaning 'other than'
Added fatha at the end to show this word is describing بَيْتٍ (house) in the phrase 'other than a house'
Root letters put into pattern to make the word for 'house'
Added tanween kasra to show this is an indefinite noun. This word is what was found 'in it' (فِيهَا) according to the ayah.
Basic preposition meaning 'from'
Added shadda because it comes before ال of المسلمين - this is a pronunciation rule when مِن comes before ال
Added a fatha because it connects to the next word that starts with 'al-' (the)
Added short 'a' vowels (fatha) to make it mean 'he submitted' in the past
Changed to active participle form showing who does the action
Added the sound masculine plural ending to show multiple people
Added the definite article to specify these particular Muslims. This word comes after مِّنَ (from/of) in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added نَا at the end to show 'we' did the action. The sukun (ْ) is added before نَا to help pronunciation.

Added fatha to help with pronunciation

steps.m: for/to you all (referring to those who should flee to Allah)
steps.n: Added كُم at the end to show this is directed at multiple people. This word connects to the command فَفِرُّوٓا۟ (so flee) in the ayah.
Basic preposition meaning 'from'
Added emphasis to the word by doubling the م sound
Added هُ at the end to show this means 'from Him', referring back to Allah mentioned earlier in the ayah
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past-tense verb
Changes from a past tense verb (he warned) to a noun meaning 'warner' by following a common pattern

steps.m: a warner (describing the speaker's role in relation to إِنِّى)
steps.n: The double-damma shows this is indefinite (any warner). This word describes the speaker's role mentioned in إِنِّى (indeed I am)
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from a past tense verb 'made clear' to an active participle 'one who makes clear' by adding mu- prefix and changing vowels

steps.n: Adding the two dots (tanween) at the end shows this word is describing something as 'clear' rather than naming someone who makes things clear

steps.m: you (plural) make/set/place (in relation to اللَّهِ - Allah)
steps.n: The suffix 'وا' changes the verb to show that multiple people are doing the action (you all).

steps.m: with/along with (connects اللَّهِ to previous phrase)
steps.n: This is a preposition that shows accompaniment. In this ayah, it connects 'Allah' to what came before, showing not to put anything alongside/with Allah.
Added ال and merged it with the word to create the proper name for God. The hamza from إِلَه is dropped in this special word.

Added kasra at the end to show possession, shadda on ل for emphasis, and changed initial alif to wasl-alif since it connects with the previous word. This word is the object of مَعَ (with) in this ayah.
Basic form of the root
Added long alif after second root letter to create noun meaning 'one who is worshipped'
Changed to basic noun pattern by removing the long alif
Added tanween fatḥa to show this is the object being taken/made in the phrase 'do not take a god'

steps.m: to come after/be different from what's before
steps.n: Basic form of the root
This form creates an adjective meaning 'another' or 'different'. Here it describes إِلٰهًا (god) in the phrase 'another god', warning against taking any deity besides Allah.
Added fatha at the end because this word is describing إِلٰهًا which has tanween fatha, so it must match its case marking.
This is an emphatic particle that makes statements stronger, like adding 'indeed' or 'truly' in English
The ن gets doubled and has a kasra (ِ) because 'I' (ي) will be attached to make it mean 'Indeed I'
Added ى at the end to show 'I' am speaking. In this ayah, the prophet is speaking to Allah.
Basic preposition showing purpose or direction

Basic preposition meaning 'from'. Added sukun to show end of word.

Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changes from 'warned' to 'warner' by adding an 'ī' sound in the middle - like changing 'teach' to 'teacher'

steps.m: a warner (one who warns others about Allah's punishment)
steps.n: The tanween (double damma) shows this is indefinite, meaning "a" warner rather than "the" warner. In this ayah, Prophet Muhammad ﷺ is describing his role as a warner to his people.
Basic form of the root
Added alif at beginning to make it mean 'he made something clear' (past tense)

This is the basic form of the word ما which can mean 'what' or act as a negative particle
Adding fatha on م changes this into a negative particle that negates the verb أَتَى (came) in this ayah
The alif is written with a special mark (آ) for pronunciation, but this doesn't change the meaning
Added fathas to create past tense verb meaning 'he came'

Added vowel marks to create a noun meaning 'before' that can show location or time

steps.m: before them (referring to those who came before)

This is a basic particle that shows where something comes from or starts, like saying 'from' in English
The shadda (ّ) is added to emphasize the word because it comes after a word ending in a similar sound. This is like saying 'frrom' with extra stress in English.
